core_meaning: To look at, watch, or see something with your eyes — but also used more broadly to mean 'to check,' 'to examine,' or 'to try doing something' (as in 〜てみる). The core idea is directing attention toward something visually or experientially.

sentences.jp: <ruby>映画<rt>えいが</rt></ruby>、いっしょに<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>ない？
sentences.notes: The most everyday use of <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る — watching a film, show, or video. Note that Japanese uses <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る for both 'watch' and 'see' in most contexts.

sentences.jp: ちょっとスケジュールを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>てもいいですか？
sentences.translation: Would it be okay if I took a look at the schedule?
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る meaning 'to check' or 'to have a look at' — very common when reviewing documents, calendars, or information.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>薬<rt>くすり</rt></ruby>を<ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>んでみてください。<ruby>効<rt>き</rt></ruby>くかもしれません。
sentences.translation: Please try taking this medicine. It might work.
sentences.notes: 〜てみる is a key grammatical use: it attaches to the て-form of a verb to mean 'try doing something and see what happens.' This construction is extremely common.

sentences.jp: <ruby>子供<rt>こども</rt></ruby>の<ruby>頃<rt>ころ</rt></ruby>、よく<ruby>星<rt>ほし</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>ながら<ruby>夢<rt>ゆめ</rt></ruby>を<ruby>語<rt>かた</rt></ruby>り<ruby>合<rt>あ</rt></ruby>った。
sentences.translation: When we were kids, we used to gaze at the stars and share our dreams with each other.
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る in a reflective, literary register — here 'gazing at' rather than casually glancing. Context and adverbs shape how intentional or extended the looking is.

sentences.jp: その<ruby>考<rt>かんが</rt></ruby>え<ruby>方<rt>かた</rt></ruby>は<ruby>別<rt>べつ</rt></ruby>の<ruby>角度<rt>かくど</rt></ruby>から<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>ると、また<ruby>違<rt>ちが</rt></ruby って<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えます。
sentences.translation: When you look at that way of thinking from a different angle, it appears quite different.
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る used abstractly — 'to view' or 'to consider' something from a perspective. This figurative use is common in discussion and analysis.

dont_use: Don't use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る when you mean to 'meet' or 'visit' someone — that's <ruby>会<rt>あ</rt></ruby>う or <ruby>訪<rt>おとず</rt></ruby>れる. Also, for reading text (books, signs), Japanese strongly prefers <ruby>読<rt>よ</rt></ruby>む; saying <ruby>本<rt>ほん</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る sounds like you're just flipping through pages without reading. Finally, don't confuse the plain verb <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る with the potential form <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える (to be visible) — <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る requires active effort, while <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える describes something being in your field of vision involuntarily.

confused_with.contrast: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る is an intentional action — you choose to look at something. <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える is a potential/spontaneous verb meaning something is visible or appears a certain way without active effort. For example, <ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える means 'the mountain can be seen (it's in view),' while <ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る means 'I look at the mountain' — a deliberate act.

frequency: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る is one of the most common verbs in the Japanese language, used constantly in daily speech across all registers. The grammar construction 〜てみる is also extremely frequent and should be learned as a separate pattern alongside the base verb.
anki_hint: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る = active looking (you choose to look); remember it has a bonus grammatical life as 〜てみる ('try doing something and see').
